title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a technology trainer called Sam talking about volunteering at a digital skills hub. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a technology trainer called Sam talking about volunteering at a digital skills hub. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A technology trainer called Sam describes volunteering at a digital skills hub.
extracts.audio_script: Good afternoon. I’m Sam, and I manage CodeConnect, a project helping local residents develop confidence with digital technology. The project is not a company and does not sell computers. We began with a few borrowed laptops in a meeting room. We now use a converted [9]bank branch[/9] on the high street; the old customer area provides enough space for teaching tables, and a former office has become a quiet room for one-to-one support. Sessions are free and available to adults of all ages.||People attend for different reasons. Some want to video-call relatives, others need help using online services. When we asked learners what they most wanted support with this year, the most common answer was [10]online banking[/10], because people wanted to understand security before managing money on a screen. New volunteers do not need advanced computer qualifications, but they must be patient. Their first training session is mostly about [11]data privacy[/11], ensuring learners' personal details and passwords remain protected while support is given. Volunteers should never ask someone to reveal a password.||During sessions, each learner works at their own pace. Beginners may use our tablets or laptops; people may also bring their own device. New volunteers usually begin by helping learners practise [12]email attachments[/12], such as sending a photograph or opening a document received from a family member. This skill is useful in many situations without being too complicated. We lend equipment in the building, but volunteers should bring [13]headphones[/13] for training videos and accessibility demonstrations, because using shared ones is less comfortable. The hub has free tea and coffee during evening sessions.||We run specialist events alongside weekly help sessions. A local photographer previously showed people how to save pictures safely. The event which filled first this term is our class on [14]smartphone security[/14], covering screen locks, suspicious messages and safe app downloads. To help people who cannot attend in person, we are producing short [15]instruction videos[/15], with large captions and simple spoken explanations. Volunteers can help check that each video is easy to follow before it is published on our website. We are also lending a small number of tablets to people completing a longer course.||Volunteers who support learners for ten sessions receive a free [16]training course[/16] in digital communication, which can be useful in employment. The hub celebrates progress each summer: instead of a formal awards ceremony, this year we are holding a [17]technology fair[/17], where learners can demonstrate skills and local organisations will offer advice. Finally, several of our laptops are becoming too slow for current software. Profits from refreshments and donated items at the fair will fund new [18]laptop computers[/18] for teaching. We welcome volunteers aged seventeen or over; details are on the leaflet beside your chair.

dialogue.speaker: sam
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I’m Sam, and I manage CodeConnect, a project helping local residents develop confidence with digital technology. The project is not a company and does not sell computers. We began with a few borrowed laptops in a meeting room. We now use a converted bank branch on the high street; the old customer area provides enough space for teaching tables, and a former office has become a quiet room for one-to-one support. Sessions are free and available to adults of all ages.||People attend for different reasons. Some want to video-call relatives, others need help using online services. When we asked learners what they most wanted support with this year, the most common answer was online banking, because people wanted to understand security before managing money on a screen. New volunteers do not need advanced computer qualifications, but they must be patient. Their first training session is mostly about data privacy, ensuring learners' personal details and passwords remain protected while support is given. Volunteers should never ask someone to reveal a password.||During sessions, each learner works at their own pace. Beginners may use our tablets or laptops; people may also bring their own device. New volunteers usually begin by helping learners practise email attachments, such as sending a photograph or opening a document received from a family member. This skill is useful in many situations without being too complicated. We lend equipment in the building, but volunteers should bring headphones for training videos and accessibility demonstrations, because using shared ones is less comfortable. The hub has free tea and coffee during evening sessions.||We run specialist events alongside weekly help sessions. A local photographer previously showed people how to save pictures safely. The event which filled first this term is our class on smartphone security, covering screen locks, suspicious messages and safe app downloads. To help people who cannot attend in person, we are producing short instruction videos, with large captions and simple spoken explanations. Volunteers can help check that each video is easy to follow before it is published on our website. We are also lending a small number of tablets to people completing a longer course.||Volunteers who support learners for ten sessions receive a free training course in digital communication, which can be useful in employment. The hub celebrates progress each summer: instead of a formal awards ceremony, this year we are holding a technology fair, where learners can demonstrate skills and local organisations will offer advice. Finally, several of our laptops are becoming too slow for current software. Profits from refreshments and donated items at the fair will fund new laptop computers for teaching. We welcome volunteers aged seventeen or over; details are on the leaflet beside your chair.

This term's quickest-filling class is about (14) .

Funds from the event will be spent on new (18) .
